data.title: China’s Xreal to power Google’s Android AI glasses as smart eyewear demand grows
data.description:
Chinese augmented reality start-up Xreal has teamed up with Google on Project Aura, a venture to launch artificial intelligence glasses powered by the Android XR operating system and Gemini AI next year, in a fresh example of Chinese capabilities in research and development.
Project Aura, resembling a pair of thick sunglasses, was first announced at Google’s annual I/O developer conference in May. The device runs on Android XR, a unified platform for extended reality (XR) devices built through a partnership between Google, Qualcomm and Samsung, and comes integrated with Gemini AI.
Aura employs a dual chip design, with an Xreal X1S chip embedded inside the glasses and a Qualcomm Snapdragon Chip housed in a separate tethered puck that also holds the battery.
The glasses feature optical see-through lenses, allowing users to clearly view their real-world surroundings with a field of view of 70 degrees. Aura projects applications directly into the user’s sightline, enabling interaction via hand gestures and granting access to Google Play apps.
“With Project Aura, we’re showing how Android XR can expand to the broader ecosystem, supporting new types of XR devices,” said Shahram Izadi, vice-president and general manager at Android XR at Google.
Xreal, previously known as Nreal, was founded in 2017 by Zhejiang University alumni Xu Chi, Wu Kejian, and Xiao Bing. It has rapidly become a major player in the smart eyewear market. Xreal One was named one of Time magazine’s best inventions of 2025.
Xreal captured 4.1 per cent of the worldwide AR/VR headsets and smart glasses market in the second quarter of 2025, placing it third globally behind only Meta and Xiaomi, according to International Data Corporation. This growth was fuelled by a 113.7 per cent year-on-year surge in shipments during the quarter, according to IDC.
Alibaba Group Holding led a US$60 million investment in Xreal in 2022. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Chinese firms are rapidly entering the competitive AI eyewear market. Electric vehicle manufacturer Li Auto recently launched its first AI glasses, adding its name to a growing list of contenders that includes giants like Alibaba, Huawei Technologies, Xiaomi and Baidu, alongside specialised players like Rokid, Rayneo and Meizu.
Global shipments of smart eyewear reached 2.5 million units in the second quarter of 2025, up 54.9 per cent year on year, according to IDC. China alone shipped 664,000 units, posting a 145.5 per cent year-on-year increase. IDC projected that total smart eyewear shipments in China will climb to 2.8 million units for 2025, up 116.4 per cent.

data.title: China may tap Nvidia’s H200 for AI gains but domestic chip strategy here to stay: analysts
data.description:
Beijing’s push to advance the domestic chip industry will remain unchanged regardless of whether it allows Chinese companies to buy Nvidia’s H200, analysts said.
Days after US President Donald Trump announced Washington would permit Nvidia to ship the chips to approved Chinese customers – on the condition that 25 per cent be “paid” to the United States – Beijing has yet to publicly state whether it will approve purchases.
So far, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs has issued the only official response. Spokesperson Guo Jiakun said on Tuesday that Beijing had “taken note of the relevant reports” and that it “consistently advocates for mutual benefit and win-win cooperation between China and the United States.”
Compared with the older-generation H20 – already cleared for sale in China – the H200 is about six times faster, boasts about 50 per cent more memory and sells for more than twice the average price, according to a Wednesday research note from Morgan Stanley analysts led by Gary Yu.
“This is an incremental improvement in geopolitical tensions, reducing the risk of a worst[-case] scenario where China lacks access to high-performing GPUs [graphics processing units],” they said.
The analysts do not expect Beijing to push back against cloud service providers buying the H200, as this would support the scaling up of China’s large language models.


“However, we don’t see this derailing China’s chip localisation – especially because local chips can still address inference computing,” they said.
On Tuesday, Trump posted on Truth Social that President Xi Jinping had responded positively to the announcement, though the Financial Times reported the same day that Chinese regulators might allow only limited access to the chips. Citing two sources, the report suggested buyers would likely require approval and that no final decision had been made.
Chinese chip stocks have continued to rally despite Trump easing restrictions on the H200.
Moore Threads, a five-year-old GPU developer founded by Nvidia’s former China chief, jumped 28 per cent on Thursday. The gain continued an upwards trend since its December 5 trading debut, when shares opened more than five times above the initial public offering (IPO) price.
The company’s IPO drew more than 4,000 times subscription from retail investors, underscoring the red-hot demand for listed companies aligned with Beijing’s self-reliance strategy.
On Wednesday, the Financial Times also reported that Beijing had encouraged the purchase of home-grown processors by Huawei Technologies and Cambricon Technologies, citing sources. The decision was reportedly made before Trump’s announcement, and after the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology added them to its list of government-approved suppliers.
DeepSeek showed that hardware limits did not stop progress
Nigel Green, deVere Group
This would be a positive development for cloud service providers, particularly giants Tencent and Alibaba, as well as data centres, the Morgan Stanley analysts said. The Post is owned by Alibaba.
The Chinese government supports domestic chipmakers as part of its self-reliance push, and providing them with a large market is one way to do this, according to Allan von Mehren, chief analyst and China economist at Danske Bank.
“At the same time, China does not want to fall behind on [artificial intelligence], so to the extent AI companies are too much at a disadvantage by not having access to H200 Nvidia chips, they may allow buying them,” he said.
Nigel Green, CEO of global financial advisory and asset management giant deVere Group, said the ability to access chips like the H200 directly shapes how quickly companies can develop and refine advanced AI systems.
Restricted access has been a major constraint in the global AI “arms race”, he said, noting that China has proven it can operate effectively under those limits.
“DeepSeek showed that hardware limits did not stop progress,” Green said. “It simply forced a different approach.”

data.title: More Hong Kong firms upbeat on economic outlook as Trump tariff worries ease
data.description:
Hong Kong companies are much more optimistic about the economic outlook for the coming year, a survey by one of the city’s biggest business chambers has found, although the organisation itself expects growth to slow in 2026.
The Hong Kong General Chamber of Commerce released the results of its annual survey on Thursday, showing that 48.3 per cent of respondents were upbeat on the business outlook for the coming 12 months.
The positive sentiment marks a sharp rebound from last year’s survey, when only 18.3 per cent of companies expressed optimism over the city’s economic prospects.
The chamber forecast that the city’s real gross domestic product growth will ease to 2.7 per cent in 2026 from an estimated 3.2 per cent this year.
The 3.2 per cent figure matches the government’s forecast, which was recently revised upwards from the previous 2 to 3 per cent increase.
Chamber chairwoman Agnes Chan Sui-kuen said: “Companies are definitely more optimistic than last year, when [US] President [Donald] Trump was threatening to raise tariffs, which created a lot of uncertainties for businesses.”

The chamber conducted its annual poll between October 30 and November 12, receiving 236 valid responses from a mix of companies, 53.4 per cent of which were small and medium-sized enterprises.
Chan noted that the geopolitical environment had become more stable.
“This year’s survey might also have been influenced by the US and China agreeing to a tariff truce and peace talks between Israel and Palestine,” Chan said.
However, she warned that businesses still faced significant headwinds that required strategic adjustments.
“Sluggish global economic growth and geopolitical headwinds remain the key challenges for businesses in Hong Kong,” Chan said. “To offset this, companies are exploring new opportunities and diversifying their markets.”
The chamber forecast that growth in merchandise exports would slow to 3 per cent, compared with a 14.3 per cent increase this year.
The outlook released on Thursday came after the US Federal Reserve cut its benchmark interest rate by a quarter of a percentage point to a range of 3.5 per cent to 3.75 per cent.
While the Hong Kong Monetary Authority followed suit, the city’s three note-issuing banks kept their prime lending rates unchanged, maintaining high borrowing costs for local businesses.
Meanwhile, the Trade Development Council said Hong Kong’s exports were expected to grow by between 8 per cent and 9 per cent in 2026, driven by demand for artificial intelligence products.
“While 2025 proved to be a year of heightened uncertainty, 2026 should be a year of greater clarity on global trade,” said Irina Fan, the council’s director of research.
“US tariffs are no longer among Hong Kong exporters’ three biggest 2026 concerns.”
Fan said business leaders were considering various strategies, including producing goods in China for selling to the Chinese market and US-made products for the American market. Some companies were even exploring a “world minus one” approach – excluding the US market – to optimise their US and non-US business portfolio, she added.
Kenneth Lee, head of the council’s special project and business advisory section, said that when it came to expansion plans over the next two-year period, Asia remained very much the focus.
For 42 per cent of respondents for the council’s fourth-quarter export confidence index, mainland China was the highest priority market, followed by the rest of Asia at 30.3 per cent and the Asean bloc at 18.9 per cent, Lee said.
The council’s index was based on a survey conducted between October 17 and November 7, covering exporters from six key sectors including electronics, clothing and jewellery.

data.title: Putin’s visit to India reveals the anxieties of a changing world order
data.description:
Russian President Vladimir Putin’s two-day state visit to New Delhi last week, for the 23rd Russia-India Summit, was heavy on optics, symbolism and multilayered signals, while tangible outcomes were relatively modest.
First and foremost, Putin signalled to the West that Russia is not isolated, despite the US-led criticism of the war his government has unleashed against Ukraine since 2022. In an unusual departure from customary diplomatic practice, three European ambassadors to India – from Britain, France and Germany – published a joint op-ed in a major Indian daily criticising Putin’s visit.
Titled “World wants the Ukraine war to end, but Russia doesn’t seem serious about peace”, this was a rare public intervention by diplomats, using an Indian outlet to influence public opinion in the host country ahead of a sensitive bilateral visit.
Russia’s ambassador to India published a response, titled “Europe’s 4 Treacheries Are Impeding Peace in Ukraine”, where he accused European governments of being the real warmongers, pinning the blame on Western interference before and during the war. All of a sudden, New Delhi became the site of intense diplomatic contestation over feckless Russian aggression.
New Delhi slammed the op-ed penned by Western European diplomats, describing it as “unacceptable and unusual”. The message to external interlocutors was that, regardless of Western attempts to isolate Putin, India would exercise strategic autonomy in the context of bilateral relations with Russia.
The second layer of signalling was bilateral. This year marks the 25th anniversary of the two countries’ strategic partnership, which was declared during Putin’s first state visit to India in 2000.

The joint statement following the summit noted that Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Putin emphasised “the special nature of this long standing and time-tested relationship, which is characterised by mutual trust, respect for each other’s core national interests and strategic convergence”.
Modi also said that, over the last 80 years, “the world has gone through many ups and downs, but India-Russia friendship has remained like the North Star”.
The third layer of signalling was for domestic audiences. The warm welcome and hug Putin received from Modi helped the Russian president burnish his image back home as a resolute leader, warding off a devious Western conspiracy to undermine Moscow, notwithstanding the heavy price the Russian people are paying for the long war in Ukraine.
Likewise, for Modi – who has been jolted by harsh tariffs from the United States for buying discounted Russian oil – the optics around Putin’s visit and affirmation of strategic autonomy in stormy waters was a necessary public show of legitimacy.

However, beyond this show of camaraderie, in which Putin showed that he is no pariah despite Western efforts to make him one and Modi indicated that India will not be intimidated by trade wars, there is an inherent fragility underlying Russian defiance and India’s quest for strategic autonomy amid heightened tensions triggered by the Trump administration.
During Putin’s visit, both sides focused on their strong military relationship, which goes back to the Cold War. However, instead of major defence deals, the focus was on cooperation in the joint production of military hardware and India’s push to boost its self-reliance in the defence sector.
Since the mid-20th century, Moscow has sent so much military hardware to India that Russian weapons accounted for 70 per cent of New Delhi’s arsenal as recently as 2022. In the later stages of the Cold War, India was officially non-aligned but also seen as closer to the Soviet camp during the Sino-Soviet split, when Beijing’s foreign policy was co-opted by Washington.
However, by 2008, India and the US had grown closer. Washington began supplying more military hardware to New Delhi as the latter sought to diversify its arsenal. Now, Russia’s share of Indian arms imports has declined to about 36 per cent. India is no longer as dependent on Moscow. However, cooperation in areas such as underwater nuclear propulsion would likely be a bilateral focus; the US has been exceptionally cautious about sharing such technology.

Coincidentally, the US published its 2025 National Security Strategy during Putin’s visit. It is clear that both India and Russia will be impacted by the significant policy shift being heralded by the Trump administration.
The ideological underpinning of American foreign and security policies at the core of the Cold War – the containment of communism and the ostensible promotion of democracy – has been discarded. Liberal values no longer reign supreme in Trump’s iteration of American power, much to the bewilderment of the European Union.
Unlike the 2022 National Security Strategy, which arguably framed China and Russia as existential threats, the 2025 version tones down the rhetoric against Washington’s adversaries. Instead, this year’s strategy prioritises US economic growth and dominance in the Western hemisphere. The document reframes global challenges through a transactional lens, calling for “mutually advantageous” ties with competitors and urging partners to align with US interests.
This Trump-driven strategy, when implemented, will have direct implications for India and Russia, particularly amid ongoing US-India trade tensions and Russia’s war in Ukraine. It tacitly prompts India to reduce its reliance on Russian energy while signalling potential US-Russia detente and a tentative US accommodation with China, which could fundamentally reshape Asian geopolitics.
The end of 2025 may well herald the beginning of a churn in major power relations. Both India and Russia, as relatively weaker nations, must brace for new geopolitical challenges.

data.title: Watch woman save dogs before escaping flames herself during fire in Philippines
data.description: A woman is drawing praise online for saving her two dogs before escaping herself from a burning building in the Philippines. Viral images show the woman tossing the animals to safety and then finally climbing down a fire ladder during the December 10, 2025, incident.\n

data.title: WHO responds to agency chief’s alleged pledge to ‘strengthen ties’ with Taiwan
data.description:
The World Health Organization said on Wednesday that its position on Taiwan remained unchanged after Japan’s finance minister claimed that the health body’s chief executive intended to strengthen ties with the island.
On December 6, Japanese Minister of Finance Satsuki Katayama made a social media post in Japanese stating that during a visit to Tokyo, the WHO director general had “explicitly [stated] his intention to strengthen ties” with Taiwan.
The post was made following a forum in Tokyo on universal health coverage organised by the Japanese government and the WHO. The event was attended by WHO Director General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus.
In one of the photos attached to her post, Katayama was shown shaking hands with Tedros, who has been accused by some – most notably US President Donald Trump – of being biased towards Beijing.
In response to a query from the South China Morning Post about Katayama’s post, a WHO media representative said that the organisation’s relations with Taiwan would continue to follow the “status quo”.
“Our position hasn’t changed. Our position is that we have existing relations with Taiwanese health partners,” the representative said.
He said a working relationship between the WHO and Taiwanese health experts and counterparts had been “going on for quite a few years now” under the organisation’s legal framework.
He added that technical collaboration with Taiwanese experts had reached “high levels” during the Covid-19 pandemic and had continued both on Covid and “other fronts”.
“We’re a health agency, and we work with health counterparts, and that will continue.”
Public transcripts released by the WHO of Tedros’ speeches at the forum in Tokyo did not mention Taiwan.
The Post contacted Katayama for comment via email but did not immediately receive a response.


Most members of the WHO are members of the United Nations, but some non-UN members have been allowed to join the health organisation, including as associate members. The People’s Republic of China is the sole legitimate representative of China in both the UN and the WHO, based on resolutions passed in the early 1970s.
Taiwan attended the World Health Assembly – the WHO’s top decision-making body – as an observer from 2009 to 2016.
It has been excluded from the assembly since 2017 due to objections from Beijing after the 2016 election of former Taiwanese leader Tsai Ing-wen, who refused to adhere to the one-China principle.
Beijing sees the island as part of China that must be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to arming Taiwan.
Taiwan received international praise for its handling of the Covid-19 pandemic and containment of the coronavirus. Michael Ryan, former executive director of the WHO’s health emergencies programme, was among those hailing the island’s efforts.
“We are observing and watching and bringing Taiwanese colleagues into the technical networks so they can share their experience and they can both contribute their knowledge but also seek new knowledge from outside,” Ryan said at a briefing held in Geneva in April 2020.
Taiwan has campaigned to be included in the assembly meetings, but the WHO member states have voted against its participation nine consecutive times.
In May, the representative offices of Britain, Australia, Canada, the Czech Republic, Japan, France, Germany and Lithuania in Taipei released a joint statement supporting Taiwan’s “meaningful engagement” with the WHO.
The statement said that Taiwan’s isolation from the World Health Assembly was “entirely unjustified” and undermined inclusive global public health cooperation and security.
Also in May, Beijing’s foreign ministry said that arrangements for Taiwan’s participation in global health affairs had been made under the one-China principle, including participation by medical and health experts in WHO technical meetings.
This includes “unimpeded and full-fledged information exchange mechanisms with the WHO and countries in the world” as well as the ability to report information to the WHO, according to the ministry.
“The so-called gap in global anti-epidemic efforts is nothing but a politically driven lie,” the ministry said.

data.title: Thailand says its military drones struck a bunker and casino along Cambodian border
data.description: Aerial footage released by the Thai military shows what was described as drones striking a casino complex and a Cambodian military bunker near the nations’ shared border.\nThai officials said the recent clashes have killed at least 15 people and forced more than 500,000 to evacuate from border areas, where fighting continues despite a recent truce brokered by the US and Asean.\n

data.title: Netflix K-drama Pro Bono: Jung Kyung-ho shines as fallen star judge in breezy legal drama
data.description:
Lead cast: Jung Kyung-ho, So Ju-yeon
Latest Nielsen rating: 6.18 per cent
Just a few months after playing a labour rights lawyer in Oh My Ghost Clients, Jung Kyung-ho is back on screens as another jurist taking on human interest cases in the breezy legal drama Pro Bono.
Here, Jung is Kang Da-wit, who starts off the show as a star judge with a massive social media following. He works at the Seoul Central District Court, which he likes to dub his castle.


Handsome, tough on corruption and generous to his staff, Da-wit is beloved by all. He skilfully feigns modesty while secretly being very ambitious – his dream is to be appointed to the Supreme Court.
However, his carefully laid plans experience a major roadblock following an unexpected meet-up with an old primary school classmate. He wakes up after a boozy night to find an apple box stuffed with cash in the boot of his car, with no recollection of how it got there.
Da-wit’s boss forces him to quietly resign to avoid a scandal and soon he is headhunted by former flame Oh Jung-in (Lee Yoo-young, appearing in her second legal drama of the year following Law and the City), a partner at her father’s large law firm Oh & Partners.
He accepts the job, expecting a plush partner gig and the corner office that comes with it, but is dumbstruck when he is guided to his new space – a cluttered pro bono office filled with well-meaning but hapless lawyers and a number of stray dogs.

The dogs are related to some of their current case work, which includes a dispute between the guardians of a golden retriever and its former abusive owners.
Still bewildered by the breathless turn of events that have sent him from a judge’s seat to a back office, Da-wit is not very focused on the case to begin with. But his ambition has not left him; recognising that Oh & Partners hopes to score PR points through its pro bono work, he strikes a deal with Jung-in.
Da-wit will throw himself into the work for a year, and if he manages to win over 70 per cent of his cases, Oh & Partners will help him secure his cherished Supreme Court nomination.
He sets to work, joining forces with idealistic human rights lawyer Park Gi-ppeum (So Ju-yeon, Dr. Romantic season 3). While Da-wit has won over his other colleagues in the pro bono department, he has his work cut out for him with Gi-ppeum because they had a run-in while he was still a judge.

At the Seoul Central District Court, Da-wit graciously came to Gi-ppeum’s aid when she was struggling with a printer, only to do a one-eighty when he realised she was not a staff member – whose votes he was actively courting.
Things quickly get complicated during their first case, when the woman claiming her dog was kidnapped hires a hotshot lawyer who Da-wit knows has a habit of fabricating evidence.
While Gi-ppeum’s idealism falters in the courtroom, Da-wit’s experience and connections succeed in keeping their case alive.
As a legal drama, Pro Bono is largely an enjoyable one, sticking to an episodic structure through the cases Da-wit needs to win and a larger frame of his fall from grace, comeback and we can safely assume his moral development.

Speaking of that moral trajectory, these opening episodes briefly let us glance at Da-wit’s rural upbringing. He may seem cocky in the present, but he had a difficult childhood and made it to the bench after passing the bar exam without a college degree.
Rather than violent crimes, the show is so far sticking to cases of social injustice, the kind that elicit tears and heartfelt victories. Look no further than Da-wit and Gi-ppeum’s first case, which goes right for the heartstrings with its focus on an abused pup.
Jung’s previous hit Oh My Ghost Clients shared a similar thematic structure, the only clear difference being that show’s supernatural premise. That said, the actor is equally entertaining here and Pro Bono offers slightly more in the way of legalese and complex jurisprudence, one of the pleasures of this genre.

Jung’s latest courtroom foray, which has already opened to strong ratings, proves that legal shows remain a top draw in the world of K-dramas.
Pro Bono is streaming on Netflix.

data.title: Hong Kong vocal coach found guilty of molesting 7 male students during lessons
data.description:
A Hong Kong vocal instructor has been found guilty of molesting seven male students over a six-year period.
The District Court on Thursday convicted Drawson Lam Chun-yat of nine counts of indecent assault and two of common assault for the offences committed in his three classrooms in Cheung Sha Wan and Kwai Chung between 2018 and 2023.
Lam, 39, was known as a contestant in the debut season of Midlife, Sing & Shine, a televised singing competition organised by TVB, which aired in 2022. He was eliminated in the preliminary round.
The instructor was arrested upon returning from mainland China in July 2023, after a 15-year-old boy from Taiwan filed a police report about being groped by him on two occasions earlier that year.
The case garnered widespread media attention and prompted 10 other complainants to come forward. One of them, a 34-year-old man, said Lam assaulted him when he was a student interviewing the instructor as part of a school assignment in 2008.
Deputy Judge Bernard Chung Wai-keung found the allegations by seven of the 11 complainants were substantiated beyond a reasonable doubt, including those made by the boy, who is now 18.
During the trial, the court heard how Lam forcefully removed the teenager’s pants and touched his genitals under the pretence of conducting a medical inspection in May 2023.
When the defendant assaulted the boy again two months later, he reportedly justified the physical contact as a necessary step towards stardom.

“You’re being groped like this because you’re not famous,” the boy quoted Lam as saying. “You’ll only become popular after being groped by others. This is very normal.”
Chung accepted the boy’s evidence and ruled the inspection of male genitalia could not be part of regular voice training.
Lam was also found guilty of molesting a 30-year-old clerk by purporting to massage his genitals in front of his then-girlfriend in 2020.
The court found insufficient evidence to support eight other counts of indecent assault and one of common assault arising from the complaints of the remaining four witnesses.
Chung said he had doubts about the 34-year-old man’s allegation, noting that he had continued to meet the defendant on several occasions after the alleged assault in 2008.
The deputy judge added that he could not be sure whether the defendant genuinely believed the complainant had consented to the contact, as the witness had neither cried for help nor attempted to escape.
The court ordered an impact assessment for the seven victims, along with reports on Lam’s background and psychological condition, before sentencing on January 26.
Lam had been remanded in custody for 26 months before being granted bail in September this year.

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest man over trying to open plane door on Cathay flight
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a man for allegedly trying to open a plane cabin door during a Cathay Pacific Airways flight from Boston.
The force said it received a report from the city’s flagship carrier at around 4.45am on Thursday about the incident.
The plane landed at Hong Kong airport at around 5am, with police officers boarding the aircraft to arrest the suspect, a 20-year-old mainland Chinese man, for allegedly violating the Aviation Security Ordinance.
The case has been classified as a “request for police assistance” and is being handled by the force’s airport district investigation team.
In a written reply to the Post, Cathay Pacific said that a passenger was suspected of attempting to open an aircraft door on flight CX811, which departed from Boston on Wednesday.
The cabin crew immediately attended to the situation, inspected the door to ensure it was securely closed, and reported the incident to the relevant authorities and police, it said.
“No crew members or passengers were injured, and the flight landed safely at Hong Kong International Airport [in the] early morning of Thursday,” it said.
According to the Aviation Security Ordinance, it is an offence to wilfully tamper or interfere with any aircraft component, apparatus, equipment or on-board systems whilst on a plane.
The maximum penalty is two years in prison and a HK$50,000 (US$6,425) fine.
